section.title: The Death of Elisha
section.sectionRationale: Israel's kings Jehoahaz and Jehoash both do evil, yet the LORD graciously delivers Israel from Aramean oppression and preserves them for the sake of His ancient covenant. Elisha, dying, gives King Jehoash a final prophetic sign about victory over Aram that the king only half fulfills through weak faith, and even after Elisha's death and burial, a corpse touching his bones is revived, a last, vivid testimony to the LORD's power at work through him to the very end.
section.thesis: Even amid Israel's persistent unfaithfulness, the LORD remains gracious, compassionate, and covenant-keeping, proving through Elisha's final acts, including power flowing from his very bones after death, that the LORD's saving power is not exhausted even when human faith is weak and partial.
section.frameNote: Four beats: Jehoahaz's oppression and the LORD's gracious deliverance, verses 1 to 9; Jehoash's reign summarized, verses 10 to 13; Elisha's final prophecy and the king's half-hearted response, verses 14 to 19; and Elisha's death, the revived corpse, and Jehoash's recovered cities, verses 20 to 25.

units.label: Jehoahaz's oppression and the LORD's grace
units.note: the LORD gave Israel a deliverer

verses.text: In the twenty-third year of the reign of Joash son of Ahaziah over Judah, Jehoahaz son of Jehu became king of Israel, and he reigned in Samaria seventeen years.
And he did evil in the sight of the LORD and followed the sins that Jeroboam son of Nebat had caused Israel to commit; he did not turn away from them.
So the anger of the LORD burned against Israel, and He delivered them continually into the hands of Hazael king of Aram and his son Ben-hadad.
Then Jehoahaz sought the favor of the LORD, and the LORD listened to him because He saw the oppression that the king of Aram had inflicted on Israel.
So the LORD gave Israel a {{yasha|deliverer}}, and they escaped the power of the Arameans. Then the people of Israel lived in their own homes as they had before.
Nevertheless, they did not turn away from the sins that the house of Jeroboam had caused Israel to commit, but they continued to walk in them. The Asherah pole even remained standing in Samaria.
Jehoahaz had no army left, except fifty horsemen, ten chariots, and ten thousand foot soldiers, because the king of Aram had destroyed them and made them like the dust at threshing.
As for the rest of the acts of Jehoahaz, along with all his accomplishments and his might, are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel?
And Jehoahaz rested with his fathers and was buried in Samaria. And his son Jehoash reigned in his place.
In the thirty-seventh year of the reign of Joash over Judah, Jehoash son of Jehoahaz became king of Israel in Samaria, and he reigned sixteen years.
And he did evil in the sight of the LORD and did not turn away from all the sins that Jeroboam son of Nebat had caused Israel to commit, but he walked in them.

As for the rest of the acts of Jehoash, along with all his accomplishments and his might, including his war against Amaziah king of Judah, are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel?
And Jehoash rested with his fathers, and Jeroboam succeeded him on the throne. Jehoash was buried in Samaria with the kings of Israel.

units.label: Elisha's final prophecy
units.note: my father, my father, the chariots and horsemen of Israel

verses.text: When Elisha had fallen sick with the illness from which he would die, Jehoash king of Israel came down to him and wept over him, saying, "My father, my father, the chariots and horsemen of Israel!"
Elisha told him, "Take a bow and some {{chets|arrows}}." So Jehoash took a bow and some arrows.
Then Elisha said to the king of Israel, "Put your hand on the bow." So the king put his hand on the bow, and Elisha put his hands on the king's hands.

"Open the east window," said Elisha. So he opened it and Elisha said, "Shoot!" So he shot. And Elisha declared: "This is the LORD's arrow of victory, the arrow of victory over Aram, for you shall strike the Arameans in Aphek until you have put an end to them."
Then Elisha said, "Take the arrows!" So he took them, and Elisha said to the king of Israel, "Strike the ground!" So he struck the ground three times and stopped.

But the man of God was angry with him and said, "You should have struck the ground five or six times. Then you would have struck down Aram until you had put an end to it. But now you will strike down Aram only three times."

units.label: Elisha's death and the revived corpse
units.note: the man was revived and stood up on his feet

verses.text: And Elisha died and was buried. Now the Moabite raiders used to come into the land every spring.

Once, as the Israelites were burying a man, suddenly they saw a band of raiders, so they threw the man's body into Elisha's tomb. And as soon as his body touched the {{etzem|bones}} of Elisha, the man was revived and stood up on his feet.
And Hazael king of Aram oppressed Israel throughout the reign of Jehoahaz.

But the LORD was gracious to Israel and had {{racham|compassion}} on them, and He turned toward them because of His covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. And to this day, the LORD has been unwilling to destroy them or cast them from His presence.
When Hazael king of Aram died, his son Ben-hadad reigned in his place.

Then Jehoash son of Jehoahaz took back from Ben-hadad son of Hazael the cities that Hazael had taken in battle from his father Jehoahaz. Jehoash defeated Ben-hadad three times, and so recovered the cities of Israel.

yasha.gloss: Deliverer or savior, from the root yasha, to save. The LORD graciously raises up an unnamed deliverer for Jehoahaz's oppressed Israel, the same root behind the name Joshua and, ultimately, Yeshua, Jesus, the name meaning the LORD saves.

chets.gloss: Arrow. Elisha's enacted prophecy with bow and arrows becomes a test of the king's own earnestness; the number of times Jehoash strikes the ground with the arrows directly determines the extent of Israel's future victories, showing how prophetic promise and human response are woven together.

etzem.gloss: Bone. That resurrecting power flows even from Elisha's etzem after his own death is among the most striking miracles in the Old Testament, a final testimony that the power at work through Elisha's ministry always belonged to the LORD, not to any inherent quality in the prophet himself.

racham.gloss: Compassion or mercy, from the root associated with a mother's womb, rechem, conveying deep, tender care. The LORD's racham toward persistently unfaithful Israel is rooted not in their merit but in His own ancient covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, a mercy that outlasts generations of national failure.

basic.context: Two more evil kings of Israel, Jehoahaz and his son Jehoash, continue Jeroboam's idolatry, yet the LORD graciously delivers oppressed Israel from Aramean domination when Jehoahaz seeks His favor, and preserves the nation for the sake of His ancient covenant despite ongoing unfaithfulness. As Elisha lies dying, King Jehoash visits him in tears, and Elisha gives him an enacted prophecy with a bow and arrows: shooting one arrow guarantees full victory over Aram, but striking the ground with the remaining arrows determines how completely that victory will be realized. Jehoash strikes only three times, earning Elisha's rebuke and a limited, partial victory rather than total destruction of Aram. Elisha then dies and is buried, yet even after his death a corpse thrown into his tomb is revived on contact with his bones, a final, vivid testimony to the LORD's ongoing power, and the chapter closes noting Jehoash recovered Israelite cities from Aram just as promised.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative recounting the reigns of two kings and the final acts of a dying prophet; it is not a formula suggesting that striking the ground a certain number of times determines outcomes generally, nor a claim that bones possess inherent magical power. What endures is the LORD's persistent grace toward an undeserving people for covenant reasons, and the sobering truth that half-hearted engagement with God's promises yields only partial results.
basic.originalLanguages: The LORD graciously raises up a yasha, deliverer or savior, for oppressed Israel, the same root behind the name Jesus. Elisha's final prophetic act uses chets, arrows, to test the king's earnestness, and the number of times he strikes determines his victories. Even Elisha's etzem, bones, retain resurrecting power after his death, a vivid testimony that the power was always the LORD's own. And the LORD's racham, compassion, toward persistently sinning Israel flows from His ancient covenant, not their merit.
basic.typology: The unnamed deliverer raised up for oppressed Israel points forward to the ultimate Deliverer, Jesus, whose name itself means the LORD saves, while the corpse revived by contact with Elisha's bones anticipates the far greater resurrection power at work in Christ, who raises the dead not through relics but by His own inherent authority and life.
basic.christ: The unnamed deliverer the LORD raises for oppressed Israel, whose name is never given because the deliverance itself, not the human agent, is the point, anticipates the ultimate Deliverer, Jesus, whose very name declares that the LORD saves, Matthew 1:21. And the astonishing revival of a corpse merely touching Elisha's bones, even after the prophet's own death, anticipates the far greater and complete resurrection life Christ possesses in Himself, John 11:25, and which He grants freely to all united to Him, needing no relic or intermediary but His own living presence.
basic.god: God remains gracious and covenant-keeping even toward a persistently unfaithful people, delivering Israel not because they deserve it but because of His ancient promise to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. He also honors even partial, hesitant faith with real though limited blessing, showing that half-hearted obedience yields real but incomplete results, while His own power, on vivid display even from a dead prophet's bones, is never in question.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 15:18-21 and 17:7-8 give the ancient covenant with Abraham that 13:23 explicitly cites as the reason for God's continued mercy toward Israel. Matthew 1:21 names Jesus as the one who will save His people from their sins, the fulfillment of the deliverer pattern raised up here. Acts 19:11-12 records handkerchiefs from Paul carrying healing power, a similar though lesser display of God working through physical means associated with His servants.
basic.oneStory: Even amid generations of persistent idolatry, God remains faithful to His ancient covenant, raising up deliverers and displaying astonishing resurrection power even from a dead prophet's bones, previewing the ultimate Deliverer, Jesus Christ, whose name means salvation and whose own resurrection life, unlike Elisha's borrowed and temporary display of power, is permanent, complete, and freely given to all who trust in Him.

context.literary: The chapter alternates between brief royal formula summaries and an extended, emotionally rich final scene between the dying Elisha and King Jehoash, closing with the striking coda of the revived corpse, a literary capstone that closes the entire Elisha narrative cycle begun in chapter 2 with equal drama.
context.historical: The severe military reduction of Jehoahaz's army, down to fifty horsemen, reflects the real and sustained Aramean dominance under Hazael attested elsewhere in the biblical record and consistent with broader regional history of this period. Reviving the dead through contact with a holy person's remains, while unique in its specific occurrence here, reflects the ancient world's broader assumption that God's power could work through physical association with His servants.
context.canonical: This chapter closes the Elisha cycle that began with his double portion request and Elijah's ascension in chapter 2, bringing the ministry that opened with parting the Jordan to a fitting conclusion in a final display of resurrection power even beyond death, while the recovered cities in verse 25 begin fulfilling Elisha's own final enacted prophecy.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that the LORD remains graciously faithful to His ancient covenant even amid Israel's persistent unfaithfulness, and to close the Elisha narrative with a final, dramatic demonstration that the power at work through the prophet's entire ministry belonged to the LORD alone, evident even after Elisha's own death.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter narrates specific historical events, including one unusual and unrepeated miracle involving Elisha's bones; it does not prescribe seeking healing through contact with human remains as an ongoing practice, nor does it establish a formula that striking an object a certain number of times determines spiritual outcomes generally. What endures is confidence in God's covenant faithfulness and the seriousness of half-hearted engagement with His promises.

debates.question: Why did Elisha grow angry at Jehoash for striking the ground only three times, verses 18 to 19?

views.label: The action tested and exposed the king's own limited resolve and faith
views.case: Elisha's instruction, strike the ground, was open-ended, inviting the king to respond with wholehearted eagerness; Jehoash's decision to stop after only three strikes, rather than continuing until told to stop, revealed a restrained, half-committed engagement with the promise, which then determined the limited scope of the resulting victories.

views.label: A prophetic enacted parable illustrating the general principle of proportionate faith and blessing
views.case: The episode functions less as a literal test of Jehoash's personal character and more as an enacted lesson, similar to other prophetic sign-acts in Scripture, illustrating a broader spiritual principle that the extent to which God's people engage His promises in faith and effort shapes the extent of the blessing they experience, without necessarily being a definitive verdict on Jehoash's entire character.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the episode teaches that eager, full engagement with God's promises yields fuller blessing than cautious, limited engagement, whether read as a specific test of this king's heart or a broader enacted principle for God's people generally.

study.originalLanguages: The LORD graciously provides Israel a yasha, deliverer, whose name is left unrecorded so that the focus remains on the LORD's own saving initiative. Elisha's final prophetic sign turns on chets, arrows, whose number of strikes against the ground determines the completeness of the coming victory over Aram. Astonishingly, even Elisha's etzem, bones, retain the LORD's resurrecting power after his death, and the LORD's enduring racham, compassion, for Israel flows entirely from His covenant with the patriarchs rather than any merit of the current generation.

typology.type: An unnamed deliverer raised up to save oppressed Israel from Aram
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, whose very name means the LORD saves, the true and final Deliverer of God's people, Matthew 1:21.

typology.type: Resurrecting power flowing even from a dead prophet's remains
typology.fulfillment: Christ's own inherent resurrection life and power, freely given to all united to Him, far surpassing any relic or physical intermediary, John 11:25-26.

study.christ: The LORD raises up an unnamed deliverer to rescue oppressed Israel from Aram, a pattern that anticipates the ultimate Deliverer, Jesus, whose very name declares that the LORD saves, Matthew 1:21, and who accomplishes complete and final deliverance rather than the partial, temporary reprieve granted here. The astonishing revival of a corpse merely touching Elisha's bones, even after the prophet's own death, testifies powerfully that the power was always the LORD's, and it anticipates the far greater and permanent resurrection life that Christ possesses in Himself and freely grants to all who are united to Him by faith, needing no relic, only Him, John 11:25.
study.god: God remains graciously faithful to His covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob even toward a persistently unfaithful people, raising up deliverers and preserving Israel's existence for His own promise's sake rather than their merit. He also honors even partial, hesitant faith with real though limited blessing, as Jehoash's restrained response to Elisha's sign yields only three victories rather than total destruction of Aram, while His own power, dramatically displayed even from a dead prophet's bones, remains entirely unlimited and unquestionable.

crossReferences.note: The ancient covenant with Abraham explicitly cited in verse 23 as the reason for the LORD's continued mercy toward unfaithful Israel.
Jesus named as the one who will save His people from their sins, the ultimate fulfillment of the deliverer pattern raised up in this chapter.
Extraordinary miracles worked through physical items associated with Paul, a lesser parallel to the power displayed through Elisha's remains.
Christ's own declaration that He is the resurrection and the life, far surpassing any power displayed through contact with a prophet's remains.

study.oneStory: Even amid generations of persistent Israelite idolatry, God remains faithful to His ancient covenant, raising up deliverers and displaying astonishing power even from a dead prophet's bones, a fitting capstone to Elisha's entire ministry. This whole account previews the ultimate Deliverer, Jesus Christ, whose name means salvation and whose own permanent, complete resurrection life, unlike the borrowed and momentary display of power at Elisha's tomb, is freely given to all who are united to Him by faith.

quiz.q: Why did the LORD raise up a deliverer for oppressed Israel under Jehoahaz?

options.text: Because Jehoahaz sought the LORD's favor and the LORD saw the severe oppression Aram had inflicted on Israel.
options.note: Verses 4 to 5 give this exact reason for the LORD's gracious deliverance.

options.text: Because Israel had fully repented of all their idolatry.
options.note: Verse 6 explicitly states they continued in Jeroboam's sins despite this deliverance, so full repentance had not occurred.

options.text: Because Jehoahaz had defeated Aram in battle himself.
options.note: Israel's army had been devastated by Aram, verse 7; the deliverance came from the LORD's initiative, not a military victory Jehoahaz achieved.

options.text: No deliverance occurred, and oppression continued unabated.
options.note: The text explicitly states the LORD provided a deliverer and Israel escaped Aram's power.

quiz.q: What enacted prophecy did the dying Elisha give King Jehoash, and how did the king respond?

options.text: He had the king shoot an arrow symbolizing victory over Aram, then strike the ground with the remaining arrows, but the king stopped after only three strikes rather than continuing further.
options.note: Verses 15 to 18 record this exact prophetic sign and the king's limited response.

options.text: He told the king to fast for seven days before battle.
options.note: The specific enacted prophecy involved a bow, arrows, and striking the ground, not fasting.

options.text: The king refused to participate in the prophecy at all.
options.note: Jehoash did take the bow and arrows and struck the ground three times, engaging with the prophecy, even if only partially.

options.text: The king struck the ground until Elisha told him to stop.
options.note: The text states the king himself stopped after three strikes, which is precisely what provoked Elisha's anger.

quiz.q: What was Elisha's response to the king's limited action, and what result did it produce?

options.text: Elisha grew angry and told him that because he stopped at three strikes, he would only defeat Aram three times rather than completely destroying them.
options.note: Verse 19 records Elisha's rebuke and the specific limited outcome that followed.

options.text: Elisha praised the king for his complete and total victory over Aram.
options.note: Elisha's response was one of rebuke for an incomplete action, not praise for a total victory.

options.text: Elisha declared that no victory over Aram would ever occur.
options.note: A real, if limited, victory was still promised and later fulfilled in verse 25, not a total denial of victory.

options.text: Elisha gave no response to the king's action at all.
options.note: The text explicitly records Elisha's anger and specific verbal rebuke.

quiz.q: What extraordinary event occurred after Elisha's death and burial?

options.text: A dead man's body, thrown into Elisha's tomb while fleeing raiders, was revived to life the instant it touched Elisha's bones.
options.note: Verses 20 to 21 record this exact, striking miracle occurring after Elisha's death.

options.text: Elisha's tomb was found completely empty with no body inside.
options.note: The text describes a body being placed into and reviving within Elisha's tomb, not an empty tomb.

options.text: Nothing unusual happened after Elisha's burial.
options.note: A specific, extraordinary revival is explicitly recorded following his burial.

options.text: The raiders themselves were struck dead near the tomb.
options.note: The miracle concerned the revival of a deceased Israelite man, not the death of the raiders.

quiz.q: Why does the text say the LORD continued to show grace and compassion to Israel despite their persistent idolatry?

options.text: Because of His covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, being unwilling to destroy Israel or cast them from His presence.
options.note: Verse 23 gives this exact covenantal reason for the LORD's ongoing mercy.

options.text: Because Israel had fully abandoned all idol worship by this point.
options.note: The text consistently notes Israel's continued idolatry throughout this chapter, not full abandonment of it.

options.text: Because Jehoash had personally torn down the Asherah pole.
options.note: The Asherah pole is noted as still standing in Samaria, verse 6, not removed by any king in this passage.

options.text: The text gives no reason for God's continued mercy.
options.note: Verse 23 explicitly and specifically grounds this mercy in God's ancient covenant with the patriarchs.

questions: The LORD raised up a deliverer for Israel not because they deserved it but because He saw their genuine distress. Where in your own life do you need to remember that God's help flows from His compassion, not your merit?

Jehoash stopped striking the ground after only three times, and it cost him a fuller victory he could have had. Where might you be settling for a smaller measure of God's promise because you stopped short of full, eager engagement?

Elisha's power to raise the dead continued to flow even after his own death, showing the power was always God's, not his own. How does recognizing that all spiritual power ultimately belongs to God, not to any human instrument, shape how you view gifted or influential people in your own life?

Israel kept sinning generation after generation, yet God's compassion for them was rooted in an ancient promise rather than their current behavior. How does knowing God's covenant faithfulness does not depend on your consistency encourage you today?

This chapter closes an entire era of dramatic prophetic ministry with one final, unexpected miracle. What faithful work has God been doing in your life over a long season that you might not yet see the full fruit of?

translationNotes.note: The deliverer's identity is left unspecified in the text; various interpreters have suggested Adad-nirari III of Assyria, whose campaigns against Aram around this period would have relieved pressure on Israel, though the text itself credits the LORD alone as the source of deliverance.

Jehoash's cry over the dying Elisha, my father, my father, the chariots and horsemen of Israel, deliberately echoes Elisha's own earlier cry over the ascending Elijah in 2:12, marking the honor Elisha had come to hold in Israel.

The number of strikes against the ground directly and symbolically determined the number of subsequent victories over Aram, later fulfilled precisely as Jehoash defeated Ben-hadad three times, verse 25.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Kings 13:1-25. No em dashes or en dashes appear in the source text for this chapter.
